findings.summary: The CometBFT profile binds the core identity, wire, native-delivery, and failure contracts without weakening the no-fallback or fatal-integrity requirements.

Selection is defined by Consensus and State Sync ownership with a required revision-derived inventory; excluded subsystem traffic, shared-route exceptions, outbound bypass coverage, and direct inbound rejection are explicit.

Send acceptance, callback status, reinjection acceptance, correlation diagnostics, disabled-mode equivalence, error outcomes, startup readiness, and orderly shutdown have externally distinguishable outcomes and boundaries.

Node instrumentation is limited to submission, callback validation, and native reinjection; scheduling policy, protocol-state observation, formal-model mapping, and a production controller remain out of scope.
The specifications preserve disabled-mode behavior and forbid changes to consensus validity, voting, transitions, schemas, persistence, timeouts, and unrelated P2P behavior.
Mandatory behavior is addressable through clause-linked source evidence, focused static and behavioral tests, acceptance traces, race checks, regression commands, and manifest consistency checks.

Revision-local files, symbols, message registrations, send and receive paths, identities, routes, codecs, lifecycle hooks, and verification commands are correctly deferred to evidence-backed repository analysis with an explicit stop-on-ambiguity rule.

assumptions: RFC 2119 and RFC 8174 normative terms have their standard meanings.
Repository-analysis and implementation phases will use the exact checked-out CometBFT revision and will stop rather than guess if its bindings contradict the specifications.
Static inspection, focused tests, structured diagnostics and metrics, process exit status, and manifest validation are acceptable forms of observable conformance evidence where appropriate.
unresolved_risks: The checked-out revision may expose inseparable shared routes or send bypasses; the mandated repository binding analysis must establish feasibility before production edits.
The checked-out revision's native API return types and lifecycle ownership may constrain how fatal errors reach the command runner while preserving disabled-mode behavior.
Exact repository-supported formatting, static-analysis, race, and regression commands remain intentionally unknown until the authorized source-analysis phase.
